evidence: LogDiceCalculator.compute returns Double.NaN (UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel) when headwordFreq or collocateTotal <= 0; the javadoc documents this and says callers should use Double.isNaN(). However in CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates the result is used in 'if (logDice >= minLogDice)' without an explicit NaN check — NaN comparisons silently return false, so results are filtered out, but the contract is honored implicitly rather than explicitly

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig javadoc states it 'Sends a 400 error response and returns null if unknown or misconfigured'. The Java return type is RelationConfig (no @Nullable annotation), requiring callers to check for null manually — the null-return contract is documented in prose but not in the type system

ComparisonResult.buildEdges() is documented 'performs list construction on every call' and never returns null. Yet ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison guards with 'if (result.getEdges() != null)' implying the old getEdges() could return null — it never could, as buildEdges() always returns a new ArrayList. The code's defensive assumption contradicts the documented contract

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() is a factory method whose documented behavior is 'Load grammar config from system property (default: grammars/relations.json)' but it re-throws IOException as IllegalStateException — callers who know it's a factory may not expect unchecked exceptions

QueryExecutor.findCollocations javadoc documents that IllegalArgumentException is thrown for unrecognized pattern format — this is enforced in BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution and correctly propagated

RelationConfig.getFullPattern(String) can receive a null or blank headword and still returns the raw pattern without substitution — no exception is thrown, no javadoc warns of this behavior; callers may be surprised by unsubstituted patterns
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch declares 'String' return type but can return null — there is no @Nullable annotation and the method is package-private, so risk is contained

strengths: LogDiceCalculator.UNCOMPUTABLE sentinel is explicitly documented with clear guidance to use Double.isNaN()
QueryExecutor interface has detailed javadoc specifying which parser each method uses and what exceptions are thrown
ComparisonResult and ExplorationResult both document that buildEdges() performs list construction on every call — the rename communicates the side-effect cost
RelationEdgeType.label() vs name() distinction is clearly documented to prevent API consumers from coupling to Java enum naming
contract_coherence.issue_character: Contracts are generally well-documented in javadoc, but a few method return-type and null-handling conventions break the stated contract at the call site — particularly the null-return from resolveRelationConfig and the defensive null-check on a return value that can never be null.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase has clearly invested in javadoc quality for its public-facing contracts. The main failures are: a null-return pattern on a non-annotated return type (resolveRelationConfig), a null-check that contradicts documented non-null behavior (getEdges), and an implicit NaN-silence pattern in logDice filtering. These are real gaps but not severe — most contracts are honored in practice, just not expressible from the type system alone. Score sits in the mid-70s.

issues.summary: resolveRelationConfig returns null as an error signal but return type has no @Nullable annotation
evidence: resolveRelationConfig is declared as 'private RelationConfig resolveRelationConfig(...)' with a non-nullable return type
The method's javadoc says it 'Sends a 400 error response and returns null if the relation is unknown or misconfigured'
Both callers check for null manually: 'if (resolvedConfig == null) return;' — null is a first-class error sentinel here but is invisible in the type signature
issues.suggestion: Add a @Nullable annotation (from javax.annotation or org.jetbrains.annotations) to the return type, or change the method to return Optional<RelationConfig> to make the nullable contract explicit at the type level. If annotation support is not available, at minimum add a @return javadoc clause documenting the null case.

issues.summary: Null-guard on getEdges() contradicts documented guarantee that buildEdges() never returns null
evidence: ComparisonResult.buildEdges() is documented and implemented to always return 'new ArrayList<>()' — it cannot return null
ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison at line 190: 'if (result.getEdges() != null)' — this null check is dead code that misrepresents the contract
The null check was likely written defensively before the buildEdges() rename, but now signals to readers that null is a possibility when it is not
issues.suggestion: Remove the 'if (result.getEdges() != null)' guard and switch the call from result.getEdges() to result.buildEdges(). The list iteration is safe unconditionally. This also completes the migration to the non-deprecated API.

issues.summary: LogDiceCalculator.compute NaN sentinel is silently handled via comparison semantics rather than explicit check
evidence: LogDiceCalculator javadoc: 'Callers should use Double.isNaN(double) to detect this state'
CollocateQueryHelper.buildAndRankCollocates uses the result directly: 'if (logDice >= minLogDice)' without a Double.isNaN check
NaN >= any double is always false in IEEE 754, so NaN results are silently filtered out without the code honoring the documented isNaN() contract
issues.suggestion: Either add an explicit 'if (Double.isNaN(logDice)) continue;' before the threshold check in buildAndRankCollocates to honor the documented contract, or change LogDiceCalculator.compute to return a dedicated low (e.g., -1.0 or 0.0) value instead of NaN so callers do not need IEEE 754 knowledge.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.getFullPattern silently returns unsubstituted pattern when headword is null/blank
evidence: getFullPattern(String headword): 'if (headword == null || headword.isBlank()) return pattern;' — returns the raw template with no substitution
No javadoc documents this no-op behavior for null/blank input
Callers receive a pattern string that still contains the original POS constraints without any lemma substitution, which will produce wrong query results
issues.suggestion: Document the null/blank no-op behavior explicitly in the javadoc: '@param headword the lemma to substitute; if null or blank, the unmodified pattern is returned'. Alternatively, throw IllegalArgumentException for null/blank headword to surface misuse early and prevent silent bad queries.

root_causes: Java lacks first-class nullable type annotations in the standard library, so null-return contracts in resolveRelationConfig are invisible at the call site
The getEdges() → buildEdges() rename left defensive code written for the old pattern in place
